item.title: Australia Seals Jet Fuel Cooperation Deal with China Amid Hormuz Crisis
item.description: Australian Foreign Minister Penny Wong announced China has agreed to cooperate on jet fuel exports, potentially loosening restrictions imposed during supply disruptions from the Iran war. The deal came during Wong's diplomatic tour of Northeast Asia.

item.title: Hezbollah Deploys Fiber-Optic Drones Adopted from Ukraine War
item.description: Hezbollah has introduced small drones controlled by fiber-optic cables that evade electronic detection, a technology first pioneered in Ukraine. The devices represent a new threat to Israeli defenses in the ongoing conflict.

item.title: Germany Overtakes UK as Europe's Largest Defense Spender
item.description: Germany has become Europe's top defense investor with 153 major projects underway, surpassing the UK. The Bundeswehr aims to become the "strongest conventional armed force in Europe" amid growing security concerns.

item.title: China replaces agriculture minister in second top-level shake-up in two years
item.description: Zhang Zhu appointed as agriculture minister following Communist Party leadership changes, marking the second major personnel shift at the ministry in two years amid ongoing agricultural policy reforms.

item.title: White House silent as China ramps up trade leverage before Trump-Xi summit
item.description: Beijing introduced new trade rules that alarmed US businesses. Analysts say the measures could undercut American efforts to reduce supply chain dependence on China ahead of the planned summit.

item.title: Kim Jong Un Praises North Korean Soldier Suicides in Ukraine War
item.description: At a ceremony unveiling a bronze sculpture of North Korean and Russian soldiers, Kim Jong Un explicitly praised troops who chose death over capture in Ukraine, the most direct acknowledgment yet of Pyongyang battlefield doctrine.

item.title: US Seeks International Coalition to Reopen Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The State Department is pressing for an international coalition to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, according to a cable seen by Reuters, as oil prices surge on fears of prolonged supply disruption.

item.title: Russian Shadow Airlines Use Algeria as Base for Secretive Military Missions
item.description: Algeria has become a key hub for Russian military logistics as shadow airlines conduct covert missions, raising concerns about Moscow's expanding influence in North Africa.

item.title: Countries Pay Iran Transit Fees to Pass Through Strait of Hormuz Despite US Sanctions
item.description: China, India, Japan and other nations continue paying Iran transit fees to ship oil through the Strait of Hormuz, circumventing US sanctions and providing Tehran with revenue.

item.title: US Eyes First Combat Deployment of Hypersonic Missile Against Iran
item.description: The US military is considering deploying hypersonic missiles against Iranian targets, which would mark the first combat use of the weapons system that has not been declared fully operational.

item.title: Eurozone Economic Growth Drops to Near Zero as Energy Costs Surge
item.description: The eurozone economy barely grew in the first quarter of 2026 as soaring energy costs from the Middle East war took their toll on European industry and consumer spending.

item.title: Russia Dismisses Price War Fears After UAE Exits OPEC+
item.description: Moscow reaffirmed its commitment to OPEC+ and rejected concerns about a price war following the UAE's surprise departure from the oil production alliance.

item.title: Supreme Court Hollows Out Voting Rights Act After 61 Years
item.description: The U.S. Supreme Court knocked out a major pillar of the Voting Rights Act, eliminating protections against racial discrimination in voting and redistricting that had stood for six decades. Civil rights advocates warn the decision could return minority communities to the Jim Crow era.

item.title: US-Iran War Has Cost $25 Billion So Far, Pentagon Official Says
item.description: A Pentagon official provided the first official cost estimate for the US military campaign against Iran, revealing the conflict has already consumed $25 billion as the war enters its third month.

item.title: Over 1 Million in Lebanon Face Acute Food Insecurity Crisis
item.description: A global hunger monitor warned that more than 1 million people in Lebanon will face acute food insecurity in coming months due to renewed conflict and mass displacement, compounding the humanitarian crisis in the region.

item.title: UK Declares Antisemitism Emergency After Double Stabbing Attack
item.description: British government announces millions in new funding to combat antisemitism following a stabbing incident, labeling the situation an emergency.

item.title: First US-Venezuela Commercial Flight in 7 Years Set to Land in Caracas
item.description: Direct commercial flights between the United States and Venezuela resume after a seven-year hiatus, marking a significant diplomatic development.

item.title: Media Organizations Demand Israel Allow Foreign Journalists into Gaza
item.description: Leaders of major media companies urge Israel to lift its ban on independent foreign journalist access to Gaza, citing press freedom concerns.

item.title: Powell Closes Eight Years at Fed Helm With Integrity Focus
item.description: Jerome Powell concludes eight-year tenure as Fed Chair spanning three administrations, 66 rate meetings, a pandemic, and criminal investigations.

item.title: Ukraine Commander Orders Mandatory 2-Month Rotation for Frontline Troops
item.description: General Syrskyi ordered mandatory rotation for soldiers after 2 months on the front lines, addressing reports of troops spending over 100 days in position without relief due to manpower shortages and deadly drone-saturated battlefields.

item.title: Middle East Airlines See 60% Drop in Demand as Iran War Disrupts Airspace
item.description: IATA reports Middle Eastern carriers suffered a 60.8% year-on-year decline in demand due to the US-Israel-Iran war closing regional airspace, with capacity down 56.9%. Only Oman and Saudi west coast routes remain operational.

item.title: Pakistan Opens Six Land Corridors to Iran, Undermining US Blockade
item.description: Pakistan formalized six overland transit routes to Iran under the Transit of Goods Order 2026, enabling cargo to bypass the US naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. Analysts call it a "double game" as Islamabad simultaneously hosts US-Iran talks.

item.title: Wall Street Giants Compete for Shell's $15B LNG Canada Stake
item.description: KKR, Apollo, and Blackstone are vying for a portion of Shell's 40% stake in the $40 billion LNG Canada project, North America's first Pacific LNG export facility. The asset sale follows Shell's $16.4B acquisition of ARC Resources.

item.title: Oil Surges to Highest Since 2022 as US Military Prepares New Iran Strike Plans
item.description: Brent crude jumped nearly 7% to over $126 per barrel after reports that US Central Command has prepared plans for "short and powerful" strikes on Iran. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed, disrupting about a fifth of global energy supplies.

item.title: US Indicts 10 Mexican Officials Including Sinaloa Governor for Drug Trafficking
item.description: Federal prosecutors in New York charged Sinaloa Governor Ruben Rocha Moya and nine other Mexican officials with aiding the Sinaloa Cartel. The indictment alleges they protected cartel leaders, provided law enforcement intel, and received millions in drug money.
